attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

claim-office.md
---JOURNAL---
# TDD Journal
1 R | empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only) | comp:OK | run:OK | -
1 G | hardcoded return { premium: 5 } | passing:1
2 R | single sword → premium 105 G | comp:OK | run:OK | function already exists, no compile error; straight to assertion as predicted
2 G | if empty→{premium:5} else hardcoded {premium:105} | passing:2
3 R | single amulet → premium 65 G (60 base + 5 fee) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; straight to assertion as predicted
3 G | added if type==="amulet" → {premium:65}, sword/default still 105 | passing:3
4 R | single staff → premium 85 G (80 base + 5 fee) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; staff hits default branch returning 105, straight to assertion as predicted
4 G | added if type==="staff" → {premium:85}, sword/default still 105 | passing:4
5 R | single potion → premium 45 G (40 base + 5 fee) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; potion hits default branch returning 105, straight to assertion as predicted
5 G | added if type==="potion" → {premium:45}, sword/default still 105 | passing:5
6 R | single rune component → premium 30 G (25 base + 5 fee) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; rune not in BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE, falls to default 100→105, straight to assertion as predicted
6 G | added rune:25 to BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE | passing:6
7 R | 2 runes → 50 G base premium (no block) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; impl only reads items[0] so prices single rune (30), straight to assertion as predicted
7 G | added if items.length===2 → {premium:55}, single-item path unchanged | passing:7
8 R | 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block of exactly 3) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; impl reads only items[0] so prices single rune (30), length!==2 so fake skipped, straight to assertion as predicted
8 G | added if items.length===3 → {premium:65}, other paths unchanged | passing:8
9 R | 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block; block requires exactly 3) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; length 4 misses fake table (only 2,3), falls to single-item path reading items[0] rune→30, straight to assertion as predicted
9 G | removed fake count-table; real per-item base sum + block-of-exactly-3 (60G) rule, +fee | passing:9
10 R | 7 runes → 175 G base premium (7×25; not exactly 3, all singles) | comp:OK | run:OK | no compile error; existing block-of-exactly-3 logic already prices 7 runes as singles (175)+fee=180, test PASSES on activation (unavoidable, as predicted)
11 R | 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (different types, no block) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists, no compile error; moonstone missing from BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE so defaults to 100 → 50+100+5=155, straight to assertion as predicted
11 G | added moonstone:25 to BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE | passing:11
12 R | 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks of 60) | comp:OK | run:OK | no compile error; existing groupByType + block-of-exactly-3 already prices two separate per-type blocks (60+60)+5=125, test PASSES on activation (unavoidable, as predicted)
13 R | cursed sword → curse adds 50% of item base (100 → +50 base premium) | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists & Item.cursed already declared, no compile error; impl ignores cursed flag → sword 100+5fee=105, straight to assertion as predicted
13 G | cursed item adds 50% of own base in basePremiumFor; enchantment untouched | passing:13
14 R | sword enchantment exactly 5 → +30% high-ench surcharge → premium 135 G | comp:OK | run:OK | function exists & Item.enchantment already declared, no compile error; impl ignores enchantment → sword 100+5fee=105, straight to assertion as predicted
14 G | enchantment===5 literal branch adds 30% of own base; curse path unchanged | passing:14
15 R | sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge | comp:OK | run:OK | no compile error; existing literal enchantment===5 check misses ench 4 → sword 100+5fee=105, test PASSES on activation (unavoidable, as predicted)
16 R | cursed sword with enchantment ≥5 → both curse and high-ench surcharges apply | comp:OK | run:OK | function/types exist, no compile error; if/else short-circuits (ench===5 branch returns 130, curse skipped) → 135 vs expected 185, straight to assertion as predicted
16 G | changed early-returns to accumulation: both ench and curse surcharges add to premium | passing:16
17 R | customer with exactly 2 years → 20% loyalty discount applies (policy-wide) | comp:FAIL | run:OK | comp prediction wrong: esbuild strips types, extra yearsWithMHPCO field caused no compile error; ran straight to assertion, loyalty ignored 105 vs expected 85 as predicted
17 G | added yearsWithMHPCO?:number to QuoteInput; if yearsWithMHPCO>=2 multiply policy base by 0.8 before fee | passing:17
18 R | first insurance → +10% surcharge per item base premium | comp:FAIL | run:OK | comp prediction wrong: esbuild strips types, extra firstInsurance field caused no compile error (same as cycle 17); ran straight to assertion, firstInsurance ignored 105 vs expected 115 as predicted
18 G | added firstInsurance?:boolean; if firstInsurance add 10% of basePremium before fee | passing:18
19 R | second quote in scenario → 15% follow-up discount applies policy-wide | comp:FAIL | run:OK | comp prediction wrong: esbuild strips types, extra followUp field caused no compile error (same as cycles 17,18); ran straight to assertion, followUp ignored 105 vs expected 90 as predicted
19 G | added followUp?:boolean; if followUp subtract 15% of basePremium before fee | passing:19
20 R | cursed sword + plain amulet → 210 before fee (curse on sword base only) | comp:OK | run:OK | function/types exist, no compile error; existing per-item curse in basePremiumFor already scopes surcharge to sword group (150)+amulet(60)=210+5fee=215, test PASSES on activation (unavoidable, as predicted)

content.content:
1	# Example Mapping: MHPCO Claim Office
2	
3	Feature for `src/claim-office.ts` (logic) + `src/cli.ts` (CLI). Tests in
4	`src/claim-office.spec.ts`.
5	
6	Two operations: **quote** (premium for a list of items) and **claim**
7	(payout for a damage report against an existing policy).
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Rules
12	
13	### R1 — Item base premiums & insurance values
14	| type      | insurance value | base premium |
15	|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
16	| sword     | 1000 G          | 100 G        |
17	| amulet    | 600 G           | 60 G         |
18	| staff     | 800 G           | 80 G         |
19	| potion    | 400 G           | 40 G         |
20	| component (rune, moonstone) | 250 G each | 25 G each |
21	
22	### R2 — Component block
23	A group of **exactly 3 alike** components (same type) has a special base
24	premium of 60 G (instead of 3×25=75). "Alike" = exactly the same type
25	(rune ≠ moonstone). Block applies only at exactly 3; 4+ alike are priced
26	as singles. Insurance value/sum is unaffected by the block (still 250 G
27	each).
28	
29	### R3 — Premium modifiers
30	- **Cursed**: +50% surcharge — item-specific (on that item's base premium).
31	- **High enchantment** (level ≥ 5): +30% surcharge — item-specific.
32	- **Loyalty** (≥ 2 years with MHPCO): −20% discount — policy-wide.
33	- **First insurance**: +10% surcharge — applied per item; each item in a
34	  quote is always treated as a first insurance regardless of customer
35	  history.
36	- **Follow-up contract**: −15% discount — policy-wide; applies to every
37	  quote after the customer's first quote in the scenario.
38	- **Processing fee**: +5 G flat, added at the very end.
39	
40	Item-specific modifiers apply to the affected item's base premium.
41	Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, follow-up) apply to the policy base
42	premium = sum of all item base premiums. First insurance is +10% per
43	item (10% of each item's base premium). Fee added last.
44	
45	### R4 — Rounding
46	Only the final amount is rounded. Premium rounds **UP**; payout rounds
47	**DOWN** (always in MHPCO's favor). Intermediate values kept as fractions.
48	
49	### R5 — Claim processing
50	- 100 G deductible per damage event (per damaged item entry).
51	- Payout per policy capped at **2× insurance sum** (based on unmodified
52	  insurance values; premium modifiers do not raise the cap).
53	- Enchantment ≥ 8: damage reimbursed at 50% of damage amount, then deductible.
54	- Dragon material: fully reimbursed, then deductible.
55	- If both apply (dragon + ench ≥ 8): the 50% rule wins.
56	- Components (rune/moonstone) have no enchantment/material → no special clause.
57	- Cap persists across successive claims on the same policy.
58	
59	### R6 — Error cases (CLI: non-zero exit + stderr, no results on stdout)
60	- Unknown item type in a quote (e.g. "broomstick").
61	- Claim references a damage entry whose item is not in the policy
62	  (wrong type, or unknown type).
63	- Damage entry with negative amount (e.g. -200).
64	- More damage entries of a given type than the policy insures.
65	
66	### R7 — CLI / scenario
67	- `claim-office` CLI at `src/cli.ts` reads JSON scenario from stdin,
68	  writes `{results: [...]}` to stdout.
69	- Scenario: `{customer:{yearsWithMHPCO}, steps:[...]}`.
70	- Steps processed sequentially; a `claim` references the quote step that
71	  created its policy via zero-based `policy` index.
72	- `results` array same length/order as `steps`.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## Examples (with expected values)
77	
78	### Block (R2)
79	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
80	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block)
81	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block; needs exactly 3)
82	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium (7×25; not exactly 3, no block)
83	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block, different types)
84	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks: 60 + 60)
85	
86	### Modifier scope (R3)
87	- cursed sword (base 100) + plain amulet (base 60) → policy base 160 G;
88	  curse adds 50 G (50% of sword base) → 210 G before further modifiers & fee.
89	
90	### Modifier thresholds (R3, R5)
91	- exactly 2 years → loyalty applies.
92	- sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-enchantment surcharge applies.
93	- sword enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge.
94	- dragon sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 → payout 400 G
95	  (50% rule wins: 500 − 100 deductible).
96	
97	### Deductible per event (R5)
98	- dragon attack damages sword (500) + amulet (300) → payout 600 G
99	  (400 + 200, deductible once per damaged item).
100	
101	### Standard reimbursement (R5)
102	- steel sword, ench 3, damage 500 → payout 400 G.
103	- rune (value 250), damage 200 → payout 100 G (200 − 100; no clause).
104	
105	### Enchantment threshold vs dragon material (R5)
106	- dragon sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (50% wins: 500 − 100).
107	- dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → payout 700 G (dragon only: 800 − 100).
108	- steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (high-ench: 500 − 100).
109	
110	### Multiple items of same type (R1, R5, R6)
111	- two swords → insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G.
112	- dragon attack with two sword damage entries → each its own deductible.
113	- more sword damage entries than swords insured → CLI non-zero exit, claim rejected.
114	
115	### Cap (R5)
116	- sword + amulet → insurance sum 1600 G, cap 3200 G.
117	- cursed sword (premium with modifiers 165 G) → cap 2000 G (unmodified value).
118	- sword + 3 runes (block) → insurance sum 1750 G (1000 + 3×250); block
119	  affects premium only.
120	- sword (sum 1000, cap 2000), two successive claims of 1500 each:
121	  - first claim → payout 1400 G (1500 − 100), cap remaining 600 G.
122	  - second claim → payout 600 G (desired 1400 capped to remaining), cap remaining 0 G.
123	
124	### Rounding (R4)
125	- premium 197.5 G → 198 G (up).
126	- payout 350.5 G → 350 G (down).
127	
128	### Edge cases (R1, R6)
129	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the fee).
130	- quote with unknown type (broomstick) → CLI non-zero exit + stderr, no results.
131	- claim references item not in policy (amulet damaged, only sword insured) →
132	  CLI non-zero exit + stderr.
133	- claim damage amount -200 → CLI non-zero exit + stderr.
134	
135	### Integration examples
136	- **Newcomer cursed sword** (0 years, no prior contract; steel, ench 3):
137	  premium 165 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first-insurance = 160 + 5 fee.
138	- **Long-standing 2nd contract** (3 years, 2nd quote; cursed sword steel ench 7):
139	  premium 160 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench − 20 loyalty
140	  + 10 first-insurance − 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee.
141	
142	---
143	
144	## Interface contract
145	
146	**Input (stdin), verbatim field names:**
147	```json
148	{
149	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
150	  "steps": [
151	    {"op": "quote", "items": [
152	      {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
153	    ]},
154	    {"op": "claim", "policy": 0, "incident": {
155	      "cause": "fire",
156	      "damages": [{"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}]
157	    }}
158	  ]
159	}
160	```
161	
162	**Output (stdout):**
163	```json
164	{"results": [{"premium": 71}, {"payout": 100, "remainingCap": 1100}]}
165	```
166	- quote result: `{premium: <integer>}`.
167	- claim result: `{payout: <integer>, remainingCap: <integer>}`.
168	
169	Item types: `sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone`. Item
170	fields `material`, `enchantment`, `cursed` are optional. Components have
171	no enchantment/material.
172	
173	The core logic in `claim-office.ts` may expose functions (e.g. `quote`,
174	`claim`, or a single `runScenario`); `cli.ts` wires stdin/stdout. Exact
175	internal function names are free, but the JSON field names above are binding.
176	
177	---
178	
179	## Questions / Clarifications (❓ resolved)
180	
181	- ❓ "Alike" components → **exactly the same type** (rune ≠ moonstone).
182	- ❓ Modifier scope on multi-item policies → item-specific modifiers
183	  (cursed, high-ench) on the item's base; policy-wide (loyalty,
184	  first-insurance per item, follow-up) on policy base; fee last.
185	- ❓ Two of the same item → each is a separate insured item; each damage
186	  entry is a separate event with its own deductible; too many damage
187	  entries of a type → reject claim (non-zero exit).
188	- ❓ "First insurance" → every item in a quote is always treated as a
189	  first insurance regardless of customer history; follow-up discount is
190	  separate and policy-wide.
191	
192	---
193	
194	## Per-test rationale (maps to it.todo order)
195	
196	1. empty item list → 5 G (fee only) — edge case, simplest quote.
197	2. single sword newcomer-neutral base path — R1 base premium + fee.
198	3. amulet / staff / potion base premiums — R1 each main item.
199	4. single rune → 25+fee — R1 component premium.
200	5. 2 runes → 50 base — R2 below-block.
201	6. 3 runes → 60 base (block) — R2 block applies.
202	7. 4 runes → 100 base (no block) — R2 exactly-3 boundary.
203	8. 7 runes → 175 base — R2 not-exactly-3, all singles.
204	9. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 — R2 different types, no block.
205	10. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 — R2 two separate blocks.
206	11. cursed sword surcharge (item-specific) — R3 cursed.
207	12. high-enchantment ench 5 boundary applies — R3 high-ench threshold.
208	13. enchantment 4 → no high-ench — R3 threshold negative.
209	14. cursed + high-ench stack on one item — R3 both item-specific.
210	15. loyalty ≥2 years (exactly 2) — R3 loyalty policy-wide.
211	16. first-insurance +10% per item — R3 first insurance.
212	17. follow-up −15% on 2nd quote — R3 follow-up policy-wide.
213	18. multi-item modifier scope: cursed sword + amulet → 210 before fee — R3 scope.
214	19. newcomer cursed sword integration → 165 — R3 integration.
215	20. long-standing 2nd contract integration → 160 — R3 integration.
216	21. premium rounds up 197.5 → 198 — R4.
217	22. claim: steel sword ench3 damage 500 → 400 — R5 standard.
218	23. claim: rune damage 200 → 100 — R5 component no clause.
219	24. claim: deductible per event, sword 500 + amulet 300 → 600 — R5.
220	25. claim: dragon sword ench8 damage 1000 → 400 — R5 50% wins at 8.
221	26. claim: dragon sword ench9 damage 1000 → 400 — R5 both, 50% wins.
222	27. claim: dragon sword ench5 damage 800 → 700 — R5 dragon only.
223	28. claim: steel sword ench9 damage 1000 → 400 — R5 high-ench only.
224	29. payout rounds down 350.5 → 350 — R4.
225	30. cap = 2× insurance sum (sword+amulet 1600 → cap 3200) — R5 cap value.
226	31. cap based on unmodified value (cursed sword → cap 2000) — R5 cap.
227	32. insurance sum with block (sword + 3 runes → 1750) — R5/R2 sum vs premium.
228	33. two swords → insurance sum 2000, cap 4000 — R5/R1.
229	34. successive claims first 1400 cap-remaining 600 — R5 cap persists.
230	35. successive claims second 600 cap-remaining 0 — R5 cap exhaustion.
231	36. CLI: reads stdin scenario, writes results to stdout — R7 happy path.
232	37. CLI: quote then claim sequential, claim references policy index — R7.
233	38. CLI error: unknown item type (broomstick) → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
234	39. CLI error: claim item not in policy → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
235	40. CLI error: negative damage amount → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
236	41. CLI error: more damage entries of a type than insured → non-zero exit — R6.
237

content.text:
I have all context. Now let me analyze the test to activate.

**Test**: newcomer (0 years) cursed sword steel ench 3 → premium 165 G (100 base + 50 curse + 10 first + 5 fee)

Breaking down the expected: sword base 100, cursed +50 (50% of 100), firstInsurance +10 (10% of base 100), fee +5. ench 3 is below 5 so no high-ench surcharge. yearsWithMHPCO 0 < 2 so no loyalty. Result = 100 + 50 + 10 + 5 = 165.

Let me trace the current implementation against this input `{ items: [{ type: "sword", cursed: true, material: "steel", enchantment: 3 }], yearsWithMHPCO: 0, firstInsurance: true }`:
- basePremiumFor: base 100, ench 3 ≠ 5 → no high-ench, cursed → +50% → 150
- groups base = 150
- firstInsuranceSurcharge: 10% of 150 = 15 (but spec says +10% of base premium 100 = 10)
- The current impl computes firstInsurance as 10% of basePremium (which includes the curse), giving 15, not 10.

So current impl would yield: policyBase 150 + firstInsurance 15 - followUp 0 + fee 5 = 170, not 165.

The test will fail at assertion: expected 165, received 170. The function exists, so no compile error.

Now let me activate exactly one test by converting the `it.todo` to executable code.

input.old_string: it.todo("newcomer (0 years) cursed sword steel ench 3 → premium 165 G (100+50 curse+10 first +5 fee)");

Test activated. All other tests remain `it.todo()`. Now my prediction.

🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "newcomer (0 years) cursed sword steel ench 3 → premium 165 G"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: `quote` and all input fields (`items`, `cursed`, `material`, `enchantment`, `yearsWithMHPCO`, `firstInsurance`) already exist and are typed in the implementation. esbuild (vitest's transform) strips types and does not type-check, so I predict **NO compilation error** and the test will run straight to the assertion.
- Note: Per the journal (cycles 17-19), adding already-declared fields produces no compile error. This is the same situation, so I expect to proceed directly to the runtime/assertion failure.

Let me run the test to verify.

- **Test file**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

- **Root cause for Green to fix**: The first-insurance surcharge in `quote()` is computed as `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE * basePremium`, where `basePremium` includes item-specific surcharges (curse, high-ench). Per spec R3, first-insurance is +10% of each item's **unmodified** base premium. The surcharge should be based on the sum of unmodified item base premiums (here 100), not the modified policy base (here 150). Expected behavior: 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee = 165.
